title: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
oneLiner: Heterogeneous group of lymphoid malignancies; typically presents with painless lymphadenopathy; can be aggressive (e.g. DLBCL) or indolent (e.g. follicular).

sections.Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL): **One-liner:** Heterogeneous group of lymphoid malignancies; typically presents with painless lymphadenopathy; can be aggressive (e.g. DLBCL) or indolent (e.g. follicular).
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO):**  
  - Single finding: facial/neck swelling + distended neck veins + stridor/stridor-like cough  
  - Immediate: sit patient up, give high-flow O₂, call oncology/radiology reg for urgent CT and stenting/radiotherapy
- **Spinal Cord Compression:**  
  - Single finding: new back pain + leg weakness/sensory level + urinary retention  
  - Immediate: call neurosurgery/oncology, give dexamethasone [check local policy] (usually 16 mg IV/PO stat)
- **Airway Obstruction (bulky mediastinal mass):**  
  - Single finding: positional stridor (worse lying flat) + reduced air entry  
  - Immediate: sit up, avoid sedation, call anaesthetics/ICU for airway assessment
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Painless, firm, rubbery lymphadenopathy (cervical, axillary, inguinal)  
  - B symptoms: fever (>38°C), drenching night sweats, unexplained weight loss (>10% in 6 months)  
  - Fatigue, pruritus, early satiety (splenomegaly)  
  - Extranodal: GI (bleeding, obstruction), CNS (headache, focal signs), skin (plaques/nodules)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Palpable nodes >1 cm, non-tender, often matted  
  - Splenomegaly, hepatomegaly  
  - Pleural or pericardial rub (effusions)  
  - Testicular mass (secondary)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Reactive lymphadenopathy** → history of local infection, tender nodes, resolves with treatment  
- **Hodgkin Lymphoma** → more often isolated supradiaphragmatic nodes, Pel-Ebstein fever, Reed-Sternberg cells on biopsy  
- **Tuberculosis** → night sweats + weight loss, but cough/sputum, positive IGRA/Quantiferon, chest X-ray changes  
- **HIV-associated lymphoproliferation** → risk factors + +HIV serology  
- **Sarcoidosis** → hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary fibrosis, raised ACE, non-caseating granulomas  
- **Metastatic carcinoma** → known primary, hard/fixed nodes, biopsy shows carcinoma  
- **Splitting feature:** Excisional lymph node biopsy with immunohistochemistry distinguishes NHL from HL and reactive causes
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - ECG if chest pain/palpitations (pericardial effusion)  
  - Urinary catheter if spinal cord compression suspected  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphocytosis/lymphopenia)  
  - U+E, creatinine, LFTs, calcium, LDH (elevated = high tumour burden or poor prognosis)  
  - Urate, phosphate (TLS risk)  
  - β2-microglobulin (prognostic marker)  
  - HIV, Hepatitis B/C serology (before rituximab)  
  - EBV, CMV serology if immunosuppressed  
  - Coagulation screen if liver involvement or planned biopsy  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging)  
  - **Second-line:** PET-CT (standard for FDG-avid subtypes, e.g. DLBCL, follicular grade 3)  
  - MRI brain/spine if neurological symptoms  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Excisional lymph node biopsy (core needle if deep site, but FNA usually insufficient)  
  - Histology: morphology + flow cytometry + immunohistochemistry (cell type, CD20, CD3, Ki-67)  
  - Bone marrow biopsy if certain subtypes (e.g. follicular, lymphoblastic) or cytopenias  
  - CSF cytology/flow if high risk of CNS disease (testicular, sinus, high LDH)  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** WHO classification (cell lineage, differentiation, molecular markers)  
  - **Ann Arbor staging** (I-IV, A/B symptoms)  
  - **International Prognostic Index (IPI)** for DLBCL: age >60, LDH >ULN, ECOG PS ≥2, extranodal sites >1, stage III/IV  
- **Never-miss test:** Excisional lymph node biopsy before starting steroids or treatment (steroids can obscure diagnosis)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If SVCO/spinal cord compression/airway obstruction → urgent senior + specialist review  
  - No steroids until biopsy unless life-threatening obstruction (discuss with haematology first)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Confirm histology and staging (CT/PET)  
  - Start TLS prophylaxis if bulky disease/high LDH/urate: allopurinol 300 mg PO OD (or rasburicase if severe)  
  - Hydration (2-3 L/day oral/IV) if high TLS risk  
  - Consider central line if planned chemotherapy  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - **Indolent (e.g. follicular grade 1-2):** watch and wait if asymptomatic; rituximab monotherapy or R-CHOP when symptomatic  
  - **Aggressive (e.g. DLBCL):** R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone) every 21 days × 6 cycles  
  - **Burkitt/lymphoblastic:** intensive chemotherapy protocols (CODOX-M/IVAC or similar) + CNS prophylaxis  
  - **Relapsed/refractory:** second-line chemo + autologous stem cell transplant  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline 1 L/8h (if TLS risk)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (if platelets >50, no bleeding risk)  
  - Diet: nil by mouth if biopsy planned; otherwise normal  
  - Observations: baseline NEWS, then 4 hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
*(Only standard protocols listed – consult local chemotherapy guidelines)*
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Rituximab 375 mg/m² IV (day 1 of cycle)  
  - Cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m² IV (day 1)  
  - Doxorubicin 50 mg/m² IV (day 1)  
  - Vincristine 1.4 mg/m² (max 2 mg) IV (day 1)  
  - Prednisolone 40-100 mg PO days 1-5 (dose per protocol)  
- **TLS prophylaxis:**  
  - Allopurinol 300 mg PO OD (reduce if renal impairment)  
  - Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV OD (for high-risk TLS)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Bendamustine + rituximab (if elderly/frail indolent)  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - Doxorubicin: cumulative cardiotoxicity (check LVEF before)  
  - Vincristine: neurotoxicity (avoid in Charcot-Marie-Tooth)  
  - Rituximab: hepatitis B reactivation – screen all patients  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin: reduce in significant renal/hepatic impairment – [check local policy]  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Vincristine given intrathecally** (lethal) – must label clearly "FOR IV USE ONLY" and never co-administer with intrathecal chemo  
  - **Rituximab infusion reaction** – premedicate with paracetamol, chlorphenamine, if severe stop infusion
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (urate, K+, phosphate increase; Ca decrease) → monitor U+E, urate, phosphate 6-8 hourly; treat with fluids, allopurinol/rasburicase  
  - Febrile neutropenia (neutrophils <0.5 within 7-14 days of chemo) → urgent IV antibiotics per local policy  
  - SVCO, spinal cord compression, airway obstruction  
  - Rituximab infusion reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension)  
  - Bleeding (thrombocytopenic) – platelet transfusion if <20 or bleeding  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Secondary malignancies (MDS/AML, solid tumours) – from chemotherapy  
  - Infertility – discuss sperm/oocyte cryopreservation  
  - Cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin) – lifetime dose limit 450-550 mg/m²  
  - Peripheral neuropathy (vincristine) – may be irreversible
sections.Prognosis:
- **Aggressive (DLBCL):** 60-80% cure with R-CHOP; IPI score predicts 5-year survival (0-1: 90%; 4-5: 40-50%)  
- **Indolent (follicular):** incurable but median survival 10-15 years; transformation to DLBCL worsens prognosis  
- **Burkitt:** rapidly fatal untreated; good outcomes with intensive chemo (70-80% cure)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send LDH at presentation** – correlates with tumour burden and prognosis  
- **Test for hepatitis B before rituximab** – reactivation can be fatal  
- **Consider TLS prophylaxis in any patient with bulky disease + high LDH** – start allopurinol day 1 of chemotherapy  
- **Discuss biopsy with haematology before giving steroids** – even a single dose can obscure histology for days
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving steroids before lymph node biopsy** – risks diagnostic delay  
- **Using fine needle aspiration (FNA) instead of excision/core** – insufficient for classification  
- **Ignoring B symptoms** – these define treatment threshold in indolent NHL  
- **Missing TLS** – check urate and K+ 6-8 hourly after first chemo in high risk
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Fever >38°C or any sign of infection (neutropenic sepsis risk)  
  - Shortness of breath, facial swelling, or stridor (SVCO/airway compromise)  
  - New back pain with leg weakness or bladder/bowel symptoms (cord compression)  
  - Jaundice or dark urine (hepatitis B reactivation)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Haematology clinic within 2-4 weeks of discharge  
  - PET-CT after 2-3 cycles of chemo (interim staging)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (med reg/haematology reg):**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - Suspected febrile neutropenia (neutrophils <0.5 + temp >38°C)  
  - Any sign of SVCO, cord compression, or airway obstruction  
  - GCS drop or new focal neurology  
- **Call ICU:**  
  - Need for non-invasive or invasive ventilation  
  - Haemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids  
  - Progressive acidosis (pH <7.2)  
  - Septic shock despite antibiotics

rawMarkdown:
## Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL)
**One-liner:** Heterogeneous group of lymphoid malignancies; typically presents with painless lymphadenopathy; can be aggressive (e.g. DLBCL) or indolent (e.g. follicular).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO):**  
  - Single finding: facial/neck swelling + distended neck veins + stridor/stridor-like cough  
  - Immediate: sit patient up, give high-flow O₂, call oncology/radiology reg for urgent CT and stenting/radiotherapy
- **Spinal Cord Compression:**  
  - Single finding: new back pain + leg weakness/sensory level + urinary retention  
  - Immediate: call neurosurgery/oncology, give dexamethasone [check local policy] (usually 16 mg IV/PO stat)
- **Airway Obstruction (bulky mediastinal mass):**  
  - Single finding: positional stridor (worse lying flat) + reduced air entry  
  - Immediate: sit up, avoid sedation, call anaesthetics/ICU for airway assessment

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Painless, firm, rubbery lymphadenopathy (cervical, axillary, inguinal)  
  - B symptoms: fever (>38°C), drenching night sweats, unexplained weight loss (>10% in 6 months)  
  - Fatigue, pruritus, early satiety (splenomegaly)  
  - Extranodal: GI (bleeding, obstruction), CNS (headache, focal signs), skin (plaques/nodules)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Palpable nodes >1 cm, non-tender, often matted  
  - Splenomegaly, hepatomegaly  
  - Pleural or pericardial rub (effusions)  
  - Testicular mass (secondary)  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Reactive lymphadenopathy** → history of local infection, tender nodes, resolves with treatment  
- **Hodgkin Lymphoma** → more often isolated supradiaphragmatic nodes, Pel-Ebstein fever, Reed-Sternberg cells on biopsy  
- **Tuberculosis** → night sweats + weight loss, but cough/sputum, positive IGRA/Quantiferon, chest X-ray changes  
- **HIV-associated lymphoproliferation** → risk factors + +HIV serology  
- **Sarcoidosis** → hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary fibrosis, raised ACE, non-caseating granulomas  
- **Metastatic carcinoma** → known primary, hard/fixed nodes, biopsy shows carcinoma  
- **Splitting feature:** Excisional lymph node biopsy with immunohistochemistry distinguishes NHL from HL and reactive causes

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - ECG if chest pain/palpitations (pericardial effusion)  
  - Urinary catheter if spinal cord compression suspected  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphocytosis/lymphopenia)  
  - U+E, creatinine, LFTs, calcium, LDH (elevated = high tumour burden or poor prognosis)  
  - Urate, phosphate (TLS risk)  
  - β2-microglobulin (prognostic marker)  
  - HIV, Hepatitis B/C serology (before rituximab)  
  - EBV, CMV serology if immunosuppressed  
  - Coagulation screen if liver involvement or planned biopsy  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging)  
  - **Second-line:** PET-CT (standard for FDG-avid subtypes, e.g. DLBCL, follicular grade 3)  
  - MRI brain/spine if neurological symptoms  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Excisional lymph node biopsy (core needle if deep site, but FNA usually insufficient)  
  - Histology: morphology + flow cytometry + immunohistochemistry (cell type, CD20, CD3, Ki-67)  
  - Bone marrow biopsy if certain subtypes (e.g. follicular, lymphoblastic) or cytopenias  
  - CSF cytology/flow if high risk of CNS disease (testicular, sinus, high LDH)  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** WHO classification (cell lineage, differentiation, molecular markers)  
  - **Ann Arbor staging** (I-IV, A/B symptoms)  
  - **International Prognostic Index (IPI)** for DLBCL: age >60, LDH >ULN, ECOG PS ≥2, extranodal sites >1, stage III/IV  
- **Never-miss test:** Excisional lymph node biopsy before starting steroids or treatment (steroids can obscure diagnosis)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If SVCO/spinal cord compression/airway obstruction → urgent senior + specialist review  
  - No steroids until biopsy unless life-threatening obstruction (discuss with haematology first)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Confirm histology and staging (CT/PET)  
  - Start TLS prophylaxis if bulky disease/high LDH/urate: allopurinol 300 mg PO OD (or rasburicase if severe)  
  - Hydration (2-3 L/day oral/IV) if high TLS risk  
  - Consider central line if planned chemotherapy  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - **Indolent (e.g. follicular grade 1-2):** watch and wait if asymptomatic; rituximab monotherapy or R-CHOP when symptomatic  
  - **Aggressive (e.g. DLBCL):** R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone) every 21 days × 6 cycles  
  - **Burkitt/lymphoblastic:** intensive chemotherapy protocols (CODOX-M/IVAC or similar) + CNS prophylaxis  
  - **Relapsed/refractory:** second-line chemo + autologous stem cell transplant  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline 1 L/8h (if TLS risk)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (if platelets >50, no bleeding risk)  
  - Diet: nil by mouth if biopsy planned; otherwise normal  
  - Observations: baseline NEWS, then 4 hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses
*(Only standard protocols listed – consult local chemotherapy guidelines)*
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Rituximab 375 mg/m² IV (day 1 of cycle)  
  - Cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m² IV (day 1)  
  - Doxorubicin 50 mg/m² IV (day 1)  
  - Vincristine 1.4 mg/m² (max 2 mg) IV (day 1)  
  - Prednisolone 40-100 mg PO days 1-5 (dose per protocol)  
- **TLS prophylaxis:**  
  - Allopurinol 300 mg PO OD (reduce if renal impairment)  
  - Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV OD (for high-risk TLS)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Bendamustine + rituximab (if elderly/frail indolent)  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - Doxorubicin: cumulative cardiotoxicity (check LVEF before)  
  - Vincristine: neurotoxicity (avoid in Charcot-Marie-Tooth)  
  - Rituximab: hepatitis B reactivation – screen all patients  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin: reduce in significant renal/hepatic impairment – [check local policy]  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Vincristine given intrathecally** (lethal) – must label clearly "FOR IV USE ONLY" and never co-administer with intrathecal chemo  
  - **Rituximab infusion reaction** – premedicate with paracetamol, chlorphenamine, if severe stop infusion

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (urate, K+, phosphate increase; Ca decrease) → monitor U+E, urate, phosphate 6-8 hourly; treat with fluids, allopurinol/rasburicase  
  - Febrile neutropenia (neutrophils <0.5 within 7-14 days of chemo) → urgent IV antibiotics per local policy  
  - SVCO, spinal cord compression, airway obstruction  
  - Rituximab infusion reaction (fever, rigors, hypotension)  
  - Bleeding (thrombocytopenic) – platelet transfusion if <20 or bleeding  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Secondary malignancies (MDS/AML, solid tumours) – from chemotherapy  
  - Infertility – discuss sperm/oocyte cryopreservation  
  - Cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin) – lifetime dose limit 450-550 mg/m²  
  - Peripheral neuropathy (vincristine) – may be irreversible

### Prognosis
- **Aggressive (DLBCL):** 60-80% cure with R-CHOP; IPI score predicts 5-year survival (0-1: 90%; 4-5: 40-50%)  
- **Indolent (follicular):** incurable but median survival 10-15 years; transformation to DLBCL worsens prognosis  
- **Burkitt:** rapidly fatal untreated; good outcomes with intensive chemo (70-80% cure)  

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send LDH at presentation** – correlates with tumour burden and prognosis  
- **Test for hepatitis B before rituximab** – reactivation can be fatal  
- **Consider TLS prophylaxis in any patient with bulky disease + high LDH** – start allopurinol day 1 of chemotherapy  
- **Discuss biopsy with haematology before giving steroids** – even a single dose can obscure histology for days

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving steroids before lymph node biopsy** – risks diagnostic delay  
- **Using fine needle aspiration (FNA) instead of excision/core** – insufficient for classification  
- **Ignoring B symptoms** – these define treatment threshold in indolent NHL  
- **Missing TLS** – check urate and K+ 6-8 hourly after first chemo in high risk  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Fever >38°C or any sign of infection (neutropenic sepsis risk)  
  - Shortness of breath, facial swelling, or stridor (SVCO/airway compromise)  
  - New back pain with leg weakness or bladder/bowel symptoms (cord compression)  
  - Jaundice or dark urine (hepatitis B reactivation)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Haematology clinic within 2-4 weeks of discharge  
  - PET-CT after 2-3 cycles of chemo (interim staging)  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (med reg/haematology reg):**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - Suspected febrile neutropenia (neutrophils <0.5 + temp >38°C)  
  - Any sign of SVCO, cord compression, or airway obstruction  
  - GCS drop or new focal neurology  
- **Call ICU:**  
  - Need for non-invasive or invasive ventilation  
  - Haemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids  
  - Progressive acidosis (pH <7.2)  
  - Septic shock despite antibiotics
